title: Searching for the Unexpected | The Point Magazine
gist: In a decade-old interview published posthumously after his June 2026 death, historian Carlo Ginzburg traces how his 'microhistory' method — reading archives of repression to recover marginalized voices — grew out of his family's anti-Fascist persecution, a long-repressed Jewish identity, and a disciplined suspicion of easy confirmation, and lays out his intellectual rivalry with Michel Foucault's power-centric historiography.

insights.claim: Carlo Ginzburg dates his entire scholarly career to a single moment as a student at Pisa's Scuola Normale, when he simultaneously decided to become a historian, to study witchcraft trials, and to try to recover the voices of the accused.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: Establishes that Ginzburg's signature method — reading repression archives to recover marginal voices — was a founding commitment made before he had any evidence it was feasible, which explains why he later treats archival obstacles as intrinsic to the project rather than incidental.
insights.quote: I suddenly made a triple decision: that I wanted to become a historian; I wanted to study witchcraft trials; and I wanted to try to rescue the voices of the defendants, men and women.
insights.locator: I was in Pisa, as a student of the Scuola Normale

insights.claim: Ginzburg's father, Leone Ginzburg — a Russian-born, Italian-naturalized literary scholar and translator of Tolstoy and Pushkin — was one of only about a dozen Italian academics to refuse the fascist loyalty oath, was jailed for it, and died in a Nazi-controlled Roman prison in 1944.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: Grounds Ginzburg's lifelong commitment to recovering the voices of the persecuted in a specific, documented family history of anti-fascist resistance, not an abstract political stance — context for evaluating the stakes he attaches to the method.
insights.quote: only twelve [full] university professors refused to swear the oath
insights.locator: But his academic career was stopped very quickly

insights.claim: Ginzburg only realized decades into his career, after an art historian remarked how obvious it was 'for a Jew to work on witches and heretics,' that his Jewish identity had been an unconscious driver of his scholarly focus on persecuted minorities — a motive he says had to stay repressed in order for him to act on it.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A methodological admission that a scholar's foundational motives can be invisible to himself for decades — a caution against taking any researcher's (or analyst's) stated rationale for a chosen focus at face value.
insights.quote: something which is that obvious must be repressed, in order to act
insights.locator: I still remember the moment, after I published

themes.theme: The Menocchio discovery and archival method

insights.claim: Ginzburg's central discovery — the 1583 Venice interrogation of the cowherd Menocchio, who described his soul leaving his body to fight witches for the fertility of crops — hit him with such force that he had to stop reading and leave the archive, because the testimony was as unexpected to him as it had been to the original inquisitor.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: Illustrates the core evidentiary payoff of microhistory: an ordinary, non-elite voice preserved in a repression record can be as intellectually destabilizing to the modern researcher as it was to the original interrogator — the discovery that produced The Cheese and the Worms.
insights.quote: We benandanti, we are born in a caul and our soul leaves our body four times a year and we fight in the meadow of Josephat against the witches
insights.locator: I still remember my own reaction to a document in Venice

insights.claim: Ginzburg calls his method of selecting archive volumes with no prior hypothesis 'Venetian Roulette' — picking volumes essentially at random when he had no other leads — and argues that the role of chance in research, though usually concealed by scholars in their published accounts, is genuinely important.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A named methodological move — random sampling of source material when a hypothesis-driven search stalls — that any researcher could adopt deliberately, and a reminder that published methodologies routinely erase the role luck played in a finding.
insights.quote: I find the role of chance in research very interesting. I mean, it is something that is usually concealed, but I think it's very interesting.
insights.locator: I went to Venice and I was playing a kind of "Venetian Roulette"

insights.claim: When Ginzburg's early hypothesis — that witchcraft trials were a crude form of class struggle — found quick, easy support in the 1519 trial of the peasant Chiara Signorini, he treated this as a warning sign rather than confirmation, reasoning that a hypothesis validated too easily is probably flawed.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A rare methodological confession that easy confirmation of a thesis should raise suspicion rather than confidence — a discipline that analysts chronically fail to apply to their own theses when early evidence appears to validate them.
insights.quote: I was deeply disappointed. I thought, there must be something wrong with the hypothesis, if the support can be found so easily!
insights.locator: So in a way, here, I found a sort of support for my hypothesis

themes.theme: Circularity between elite and popular culture

insights.claim: Drawing on Gramsci, Ginzburg argues that cultural ideas move in both directions between social strata: elites often articulate concepts that are 'in fact rooted in society at large and also among popular classes,' not the other way around as usually assumed.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A durable interpretive lens for any stratified society: apparent elite cultural or political innovations may actually be repackaged versions of pre-existing popular sentiment rather than original top-down ideas.
insights.quote: there is a sort of circularity
insights.locator: But, I think about the idea of a circularity

insights.claim: Applying the circularity model to Menocchio's cosmology of toleration, Ginzburg suggests the standard explanation — that a peasant simply absorbed an idea from elite written culture — may run backwards: elites are often articulating something that has popular roots, usually without knowing it.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: Suggests that apparently novel elite ideas (cultural, political, or intellectual) may be unacknowledged repackagings of preexisting popular sentiment — a check against overrating the originality of elite discourse.
insights.quote: the elites are articulating something which actually has popular roots
insights.locator: Not only that, but also the other way around

insights.claim: At a 1979 Paris seminar on Discipline and Punish, Michel Foucault introduced himself as 'pas historien' (not a historian) rather than 'pas philosophe,' which Ginzburg reads as evidence that, despite his aggressive public persona, Foucault avoided real debate and needed to keep discussion under his own control.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A firsthand character read on Foucault from a rival historian — context for weighing how much of Foucault's disciplinary self-positioning reflected genuine intellectual conviction versus a psychological need for control.
insights.quote: this aggressiveness concealed fragility. He didn't like to be involved in a real debate.

insights.claim: Ginzburg argues Foucault's obsession with power, while analytically productive, caused him to overrate power as an explanatory frame to the point of not actually listening to the voices of the oppressed in the archival record — the opposite of Ginzburg's own method.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: Positions microhistory's archival listening to marginal voices as a direct methodological corrective to Foucauldian power-centric historiography — useful framing for choosing which historical or social-science lens best fits a given case.
insights.quote: Foucault was obsessed by power, which certainly helped him to find something. But in my view he overrated this to the point of not listening to the voices of the oppressed.
insights.locator: Well, because I think Foucault was obsessed by power

insights.claim: Ginzburg's most disturbing personal discovery, in his own telling, was that the secular historian's core method — 'historical perspective,' understanding the past on its own terms — is a reworking of the Augustinian Christian attitude toward the Jews, an ambivalent doctrine that both licensed persecution and insisted on preserving Jewish presence for later understanding.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A caution that even 'neutral' scholarly method (contextualizing the past in its own terms) can carry a theological, ideologically freighted genealogy worth interrogating rather than assuming methodological objectivity is free of history.
insights.quote: the notion of historical perspective is actually a sort of reworking of the Christian attitude toward the Jews
insights.locator: For instance, I once said the only idea I had in my life

insights.claim: Ginzburg's explicit research discipline is to treat an easy confirmation of a hypothesis as a failure signal: he insists researchers must 'complicate the game' by multiplying obstacles and maintaining a running dialogue with a devil's advocate who raises hard-to-answer objections.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A general-purpose discipline against confirmation bias — treat an easy 'yes' to your hypothesis as a red flag and institutionalize a devil's-advocate check — directly transferable to testing any analytical thesis, including investment theses.
insights.quote: If somebody finds what he or she is looking for—that's a disaster. It's not enough. You have to complicate the game.
insights.locator: Yeah, exactly. If somebody finds what he or she is looking for

insights.claim: Ginzburg cites a Brecht line, recorded by Walter Benjamin during their chess games in Danish exile — 'We should not start from good old things, but from bad new things' — as his favorite motto for choosing which questions to research: prioritize unpleasant, unresolved questions over comfortable settled ones.
insights.speaker: Carlo Ginzburg
insights.so_what: A transferable heuristic for research or investment-thesis agenda-setting: deliberately prioritize uncomfortable, emergent questions over comfortable, already-settled ones.
insights.quote: We should not start from good old things, but from bad new things.
insights.locator: Yes! This is one of my favorite mottos
